original_abstract: This volume explores the persistent educational disparities facing children of Turkish immigrants across Austria, France, and Sweden. Despite being one of the most prominent immigrant populations in these nations, individuals of Turkish descent continue to encounter systemic discrimination and restricted pathways to success—barriers that are particularly visible within the school system. A central finding of this study is that institutional structures play a decisive role in shaping outcomes; specifically, countries that offer more supportive educational arrangements significantly enhance the prospects for upward economic mobility among the second generation.
humanized_abstract: Children of Turkish origin constitute one of the largest immigrant groups in the European countries of Austria, France and Sweden. Despite their large numbers, members of this group are exposed to many forms of discrimination and blockages in their striving for social advancement – especially within the educational system. The studies presented in this volume make clear that discrimination and obstacles also affect children of Turkish origin in these countries – one of the largest immigrant groups in Europe. A common theme of the studies is that of administrative conditions that affect the destiny of the second generation. The educational system of the countries in question also differs markedly and the countries with more favourable administrative conditions for the educational system have had a more favourable effect on the chances of economic advancement for the second generation.
